sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Deconditioning is loss of physical capacity after inactivity, illness, pain, or prolonged restricted duties. Recovery requires progressive, tolerable reloading rather than waiting for perfect symptoms.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Reduced walking distance, fatigue, weakness, or breathlessness with usual activity
- Reduced tolerance for work tasks after time off or restricted duties
- Loss of confidence with lifting, stairs, or longer shifts
- Post-illness or post-injury decline in endurance

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Rule out ongoing acute disease, unstable cardiovascular symptoms, severe anaemia, hypoxia, or infection before assuming the problem is pure deconditioning
- Boom-bust cycles are common and should shape pacing advice
- Very low starting capacity may still justify early reactivation with close monitoring

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Establish current tolerance and what activities provoke delayed worsening
- Encourage regular low-dose activity rather than sporadic overexertion
- Escalate for chest pain, syncope, resting hypoxia, or other unstable medical features

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Build a graded, measurable program for walking, strengthening, stairs, carrying, or task simulation based on role needs
- Use pacing and symptom monitoring to avoid major flare cycles
- Combine endurance, strength, mobility, and confidence restoration rather than relying on one exercise type alone

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - Reconditioning goals should link directly to meaningful tasks such as shift tolerance, ladder climbing, carrying, or prolonged standing
